caveats: a: No public six-digit occupation study.
a: Classified and proprietary workflows limit evidence.
rho: Bounded judgment, not observed adoption.
rho: Autonomous propulsion control or safety disposition is excluded.
e: Auto-parts margin bridge is especially weak for propulsion development and production.
e: Establishments may not equal transferable firms.
s5: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
s5: Tiny denominator causes high uncertainty.
q: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
q: Commercial fixed-price and government cost-type programs differ.
d5: Budget and testing activity are not delivered quantity.
d5: Demand is concentrated in a few programs.
o: Operator share may migrate to primes or government facilities.
o: This is distinct from labor automation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Long-cycle development and production-lot contracts with repeat qualification, hot-fire or component testing, configuration control, mission assurance, spares, and technical support.
lens.excluded_activities: Complete vehicles, aircraft engines, unrelated propulsion products, captive divisions, government facilities, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers and prototype-to-production suppliers of guided-missile and space-vehicle propulsion units and propulsion-unit parts sold repeatedly to external primes, government programs, and commercial-space customers.

a.bridge: BLS aerospace data cover NAICS 3364, not propulsion-specific tasks, and show both engineering occupations and physical aerospace assembly. NASA sources establish intensive propulsion test, certification, and quality workflows. I map engineering search, requirements and test documentation, simulation support, configuration, scheduling, procurement, proposal work, and test-data analysis as exposed; hazardous-material handling, precision production, assembly, hot-fire testing, inspection, and mission-assurance decisions are less substitutable. The broad proxy may overstate exposure under security and safety constraints.
caveats: No public six-digit occupation study.
Classified and proprietary workflows limit evidence.
a.change_evidence: Cleared payroll-weighted task inventories across engineering, test, production, quality, program management, and security at 5-7 eligible suppliers would materially revise the estimate.
a.rationale: Engineering and test information flows are substantial, but physical production, hazardous systems, and high-consequence validation limit substitution.

d5.bridge: DoD states FY2026 investment in conventional and hypersonic munitions supports nearly 2,000 critical weapons, many at maximum industrial-base capacity, while NASA maintains major propulsion testing programs. These are program and capacity indicators rather than five-year constant-price demand for eligible LMM propulsion firms. I translate them into strong growth with wide downside for appropriations, program failure, and prime insourcing.
caveats: Budget and testing activity are not delivered quantity.
Demand is concentrated in a few programs.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year funded propulsion-unit quantities, solid-rocket-motor capacity plans, commercial launch manifests, NASA mission procurement, and target backlog would materially revise the range.
d5.rationale: Missile replenishment, hypersonic and missile-defense programs, launch cadence, and space missions support propulsion demand, constrained by qualification lead times and program volatility.

caveats: Auto-parts margin bridge is especially weak for propulsion development and production.
Establishments may not equal transferable firms.
e.change_evidence: Screen all seven candidates using CAGE and SAM.gov records, DoD contracts, commercial-space awards, ownership, facility clearance, test assets, backlog, and audited EBITDA.
e.rationale: The seven-firm injected population likely includes strategic subsidiaries, captive propulsion operations, development-stage ventures, or establishments without transferable recurring production. Eligible independents require qualified facilities, cleared personnel, funded programs, and external repeat customers.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 336415 ($2,278,352,785) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 336415 ($19,875,511,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0904 (margins.json[336]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Auto Parts EBITDA/Sales 9.04%); revenue/employee $546,436 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 336415); class 100-499: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $39,879,857 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,605,139 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,262,529,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $294,932,637 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 7

caveats: Operator share may migrate to primes or government facilities.
This is distinct from labor automation.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of broad prime insourcing, government organic production, or radically different propulsion architectures eliminating specialist manufacturers would move the estimate.
o.rationale: Retained demand requires accountable specialists to manufacture, integrate, certify, and physically test propulsion hardware. Software can improve design and analysis but cannot replace hazardous-material production, test assets, or mission-assurance responsibility.

caveats: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
Commercial fixed-price and government cost-type programs differ.
q.change_evidence: Program contracts, fee histories, cost-accounting treatment, learning-curve clauses, qualification cost, and customer interviews would directly test retention.
q.rationale: Unique designs, qualification history, scarce test assets, mission criticality, and switching costs protect benefits. Cost-type development, certified cost data, negotiated government pricing, prime cost-down demands, and later recompetition pass some savings through.

caveats: Bounded judgment, not observed adoption.
Autonomous propulsion control or safety disposition is excluded.
rho.change_evidence: Audited secure-environment pilots measuring labor hours, review burden, defects, and test-analysis cycle time in non-safety-critical workflows would move the range.
rho.rationale: Secure tools can assist documentation, analysis, simulation, planning, and proposals within five years. Classified environments, cyber accreditation, sparse test data, export controls, certification, hazardous processes, and catastrophic failure risk constrain implementation.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence covers small businesses generally rather than missile and space propulsion transfers. I discount heavily for the tiny pool, strategic ownership, government consent, export controls, clearances, hazardous facilities, contract novation, and technical risk. Industrial-base expansion may prompt strategic acquisition, but many deals fall outside LMM.
caveats: Economy-wide demographic proxy.
Tiny denominator causes high uncertainty.
s5.change_evidence: CAGE ownership histories, SAM contract novations, CFIUS disclosures, and propulsion-sector M&A transactions since 2015 would create a denominator-based rate.
s5.rationale: Strategic interest exists, but scarce qualified firms and national-security, facility, and program constraints make qualifying control transfers uncommon.

principal_driver: Strategic propulsion capacity is scarce while missile and space programs require more qualified physical output.
principal_weakness: The transferable LMM population is extremely small and operational risk is unusually high.

run_meta.title: Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Propulsion Unit and Propulsion Unit Parts Manufacturing

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as manufacturing guided-missile or space-vehicle propulsion units and propulsion-unit parts.
sources.title: Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Propulsion Manufacturing

sources.supports: NASA identifies rocket propulsion testing as a major engineering and test mission, supporting physical test and accountability requirements.
sources.title: NASA Propulsion Test Engineering

sources.supports: DoD states $6.5 billion supports conventional and hypersonic munitions and nearly 2,000 critical weapons, many produced at maximum industrial-base capacity.
sources.title: DoD Background Briefing on FY 2026 Defense Budget

sources.supports: NASA specifies minimum certification and maintenance requirements for propellant and pressurant systems used in rocket-engine testing and support operations.
sources.title: NASA Standard for Propellants and Pressurants Used in Testing

sources.supports: NASA OIG describes quality-control obligations for parts used in launch vehicles and propulsion systems.
sources.title: NASA Parts Quality Control Process

sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner

title: Guided Missile and Space Vehicle Propulsion Unit and Propulsion Unit Parts Manufacturing
